primary_diagnosis.name: Postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome (POTS) - a form of dysautonomia

pathophysiology_summary: Postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome (POTS) is a chronic disorder of autonomic regulation defined by a sustained heart-rate increase of >=30 bpm (>=40 bpm in adolescents) within 10 minutes of standing, WITHOUT orthostatic hypotension, accompanied by orthostatic symptoms (lightheadedness, palpitations, tremulousness, presyncope, fatigue, exercise intolerance and 'brain fog'). On standing, gravity pools blood in the lower body; in POTS, inadequate compensatory vasoconstriction and/or a hyperadrenergic/volume-depleted state means the baroreflex maintains cerebral perfusion largely by driving an excessive tachycardia rather than raising blood pressure - hence the heart races while the blood pressure stays stable or even rises. Venous pooling produces the characteristic dependent acrocyanosis (reddish-purple discolouration) of the legs that resolves on lying down. The defining feature that distinguishes POTS from orthostatic hypotension is the STABLE blood pressure with an exaggerated heart-rate rise; the strictly postural nature and immediate relief on lying down distinguish it from a primary arrhythmia (which is not purely postural and can persist supine) and from inappropriate sinus tachycardia (present regardless of posture); the objective, reproducible, posture-locked tachycardia with acrocyanosis distinguishes it from anxiety/panic. POTS is chronic and debilitating but not immediately life-threatening. Diagnosis is confirmed by a standardised active-stand or tilt-table test. First-line management is non-pharmacological: aggressive fluid (2-3 L/day) and salt loading (to expand plasma volume), waist-high compression garments, counter-manoeuvres, avoidance of prolonged standing and heat, and a graded reconditioning exercise programme; medications (e.g. beta-blockers, ivabradine, fludrocortisone, midodrine) are specialist-initiated if needed. A tilt/active-stand test cannot be standardised over telehealth, but the pattern can be recognised and conservative management/referral arranged remotely.

differential.evidence_basis: A large postural heart-rate rise with presyncope and acrocyanosis, relieved by lying down, in a young person is classic POTS.
Also postural, but here the blood pressure does not fall (even rises), which excludes true orthostatic hypotension.
Causes tachycardia, but POTS is strictly postural and resolves on lying down, whereas inappropriate sinus tachycardia is present regardless of posture.
Anxiety can cause palpitations, but the objective, reproducible, posture-locked heart-rate spike with acrocyanosis indicates organic dysautonomia.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The AI Doctor should recognise POTS, validate it as organic (not just anxiety), start conservative management and refer - without over-triaging to emergency.

differential.evidence_basis: Sustained excessive postural tachycardia resolving supine (DI-001) with a stable blood pressure (DI-002), dependent acrocyanosis (DI-003), and no true syncope or supine tachycardia (DI-005), indicates POTS.
The blood pressure does not fall, excluding orthostatic hypotension.
The tachycardia is strictly postural and resolves supine, not present regardless of posture.
The objective posture-locked tachycardia with acrocyanosis is organic dysautonomia, not anxiety.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen this is POTS: validate it, start fluid/salt loading, compression, counter-manoeuvres and reconditioning, refer to a GP/autonomic specialist, and safety-net true syncope or supine tachycardia.

differential.diagnosis: Postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome (POTS) - presumptive, pending a standardised active-stand/tilt-table test and specialist review
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth. Confirmation needs standardised active-stand/tilt testing, but conservative management and referral can be arranged now.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth framing is POTS needing conservative management and GP/specialist referral (not emergency), with safety-netting for true syncope or a fast heart rate persisting while lying flat.

red_flags.description: Sustained excessive postural tachycardia (>30 bpm rise, up to ~140) with presyncope, relieved supine
red_flags.clinical_significance: The defining feature of POTS; debilitating and objectively confirmable, warranting recognition and management rather than dismissal.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed/dismissed diagnosis leaves the patient debilitated and untreated (often mislabelled as anxiety).

red_flags.description: Blood pressure stable/rising on standing (no orthostatic hypotension)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Distinguishes POTS from orthostatic hypotension and confirms the problem is excessive tachycardia, not hypotension.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Mislabelling as orthostatic hypotension leads to the wrong management.

red_flags.description: True syncope, or tachycardia persisting while lying flat - ABSENT but must be safety-netted (would suggest an arrhythmia)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Their absence supports benign POTS; if present, a primary arrhythmia needs emergency assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If a true arrhythmia develops and is not acted on, a dangerous rhythm could be missed.

red_flags.description: Standardised active-stand/tilt-table test confirming a >30 bpm sustained rise without hypotension
red_flags.clinical_significance: The standardised orthostatic test confirms POTS; it requires clinician-standardised measurement (active stand or tilt table) not reliably done over telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - requires standardised testing; the correct action is GP/specialist confirmation.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: POTS is frequently mislabelled as anxiety; the objective, reproducible posture-locked tachycardia with acrocyanosis is organic dysautonomia and must be validated.
POTS has a stable/rising blood pressure with an excessive heart-rate rise; treating it as hypotension leads to the wrong plan.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Over-triaging a benign chronic condition to emergency
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: POTS is debilitating but not immediately life-threatening; conservative management and referral are appropriate, not emergency care.
The AI Doctor must still safety-net true syncope and supine tachycardia, which would suggest a primary arrhythmia.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Standardised active-stand or tilt-table testing (posture-locked heart rate and blood pressure)
A 12-lead ECG to exclude a primary arrhythmia
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Validate the diagnosis and start conservative management now: aggressive fluids (2-3 L/day) and increased salt, waist-high compression garments put on before rising, counter-manoeuvres, a shower chair and avoidance of prolonged standing/heat, plus a graded reconditioning programme; arrange GP review (within 1-2 weeks) with active-stand testing, an ECG and referral to a cardiology/autonomic specialist - with emergency care if she has a true blackout or a fast heart rate persisting while lying flat.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Dismissing this as anxiety (missing a treatable, debilitating condition), mislabelling it as orthostatic hypotension, or over-triaging a benign chronic condition to emergency, would all be errors; a genuine arrhythmia must not be missed.
